settings.title: Our Sourdough Is Now Baked Every Day
settings.excerpt: After two years of weekends only, the long-ferment loaf has earned a permanent spot in the oven.
settings.body:
We started baking sourdough at Crumbly on Saturdays as a small experiment. It sold out so fast that we have decided to make it a daily loaf.

The dough ferments for around twenty-four hours, which is why the crust shatters and the crumb stays open and chewy. Nothing goes in but flour, water, salt, and time.

Why the long ferment matters

That full day of fermentation is doing real work, not just sitting around. The wild yeast and bacteria in our starter slowly break down the flour, which is where the faint tang and the deeper flavour come from. It also makes the loaf easier on the stomach for a lot of people, and it gives the crust that thin, crackling shell you cannot rush. There is no commercial yeast and no shortcut. Just our starter, which we feed every day, and patience.

Moving from weekends to daily meant changing how we run the bakery. The starter now needs feeding around the clock, and the proving schedule had to be rebuilt so a fresh batch is ready every single morning rather than twice a week.

Getting a loaf

  • Loaves come out of the oven around nine each morning.
  • We bake a set number, so once the day's batch is gone, it is gone.
  • The early risers tend to win, so come before midday if you can.

Once the day's batch is gone, it is gone, so the early risers tend to win. If you want to be sure, a quick call ahead lets us set one aside for you.
